title: Power-law distribution and concentration of clinical trials across 30 African nations
primary_estimand: Gini coefficient and power-law exponent of trial count distribution across African countries
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration of ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: Does the distribution of clinical trials across Africa follow a power law, and how does its concentration compare with other global regions? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for interventional trial counts across 30 African, 15 Latin American, and 15 Asian countries, fitting log-rank versus log-count via ordinary least squares and computing Gini coefficients and Herfindahl-Hirschman indices. The African trial distribution fits a power law on the log-log plot, with the top two countries capturing a disproportionate share while over half of countries fall below one trial per million population. Africa's trial Gini coefficient exceeds its income Gini, indicating that research is distributed more unequally than wealth itself. The Matthew Effect -- preferential attachment driving self-reinforcing concentration -- explains why incremental funding flows to existing hubs. Policy should target mid-ranked countries at the elbow of the power-law curve, where small investments can yield disproportionate returns in breaking the concentration cycle.
ai_transparency: LLM used for drafting; author reviewed and takes responsibility for final content.

checks.name: primary estimate in sentence 4
checks.detail: Sentence 4 reports power-law fit and top-2 concentration.
